coastalMinorEvents: A small glass bottle has washed ashore, sealed with wax and filled with sand and a dried sea flower — nothing else.
A weathered rowboat lies above the tide line, oars still slotted, but its hull is filled to the brim with smooth river stones. No river runs nearby.
The surf retreats further than expected, exposing an unusual stretch of seabed. Strange geometric shapes are visible in the wet sand, too regular to be natural.
A pair of seals watches the party with unusual attentiveness, occasionally barking as though trying to communicate something before sliding back into the water.
A length of rope has washed up on shore, knotted at intervals in a pattern that resembles a coded message. The knots are fresh.
Three iron anchors lie half-buried in the sand in a tight triangle, though there is no sign of any vessel and the nearest harbor is far away.
At low tide, a wooden door frame stands upright in the shallows, intact but attached to nothing. Its hinges are polished and its wood is unrotted.
An enormous jellyfish, translucent pale blue, has beached itself. It is still alive and oddly luminescent even in daylight.
Dozens of silver coins are scattered across the wet sand, as if dropped carelessly. All bear the same unrecognizable face on one side.
A figure stands at the water's edge, staring out to sea. When the party approaches they find only a pile of waterlogged traveling clothes.
The body of a large sea turtle floats just beyond the breakers, resting peacefully upside down. On its shell is carved a set of coordinates.
A hermit waves from a high sea-cliff ledge, shouting warnings about something in the water. He vanishes before the party can reach him.
Orange foam collects along the tide line. It smells faintly of sulfur and dissipates on contact — harmless, but unsettling.
A rusted iron cage, large enough to hold a person, sits at the high tide mark. One door hangs open. Wet footprints lead away from it.
A pod of dolphins beaches briefly, thrashing violently, then rights itself and returns to sea. In their wake they leave a small wrapped bundle on the sand.
A collapsed watchtower slumps at the cliff's edge. Inside the rubble, a brazier — still warm — and a signaling mirror, recently polished.
A ship's bell stands upright in the shallows. Each wave that passes causes it to toll once, though no clapper is visible.
Hundreds of tiny crabs all march in the same direction, up and away from the sea, as if fleeing something beneath the waves.
A mermaid's purse — the black leathery egg case of a large shark — has washed up. Inside is something that pulses faintly and glows.
Two large fishermen argue loudly over a net. When the party approaches they fall silent, stare, then turn and wade into the sea fully clothed.
A wooden chest sits open just at the waterline. It is filled with salt, and buried in the salt is a perfectly preserved human hand.
The mouth of a sea-cave is revealed at low tide. Painted above the entrance in red pigment is a series of ship silhouettes — one for each vessel, presumably lost.
A massive kelp bloom has beached, and among its dark fronds something glints. Three silver ingots, stamped with a defunct treasury's mark.
A bonfire has been laid on the beach, fuel arranged and ready, but unlit. Around it: twelve small stones, each painted with an eye.
A lone pelican follows the party along the beach for over an hour. It carries something shiny in its pouch and drops it at the party's feet when they stop: a small brass key.
A series of stone steps lead up from the beach to nowhere — the cliff face above them has crumbled away, leaving them floating midair.
A fishing net strung between two poles acts as a gate across the beach. Hanging from it: painted wooden fish, small mirrors, and a single dried human ear.
A salt circle has been drawn on the flat rock above the tide line. In its center: candle stumps, a gutted lantern, and a page of dense, wet script in a religious hand.
The wreck of a small skiff lies on its side, barnacle-covered, clearly old — but its cargo hold contains a crate stenciled with a merchant's mark active only last season.
A line of colored stones leads from the cliff path down to the water's edge and directly into the sea, as if someone was marking a path into the depths.
shipboardEvents: One of the deckhands is discovered asleep at his post. The boatswain dresses him down in front of the crew, but the man insists he was not sleeping — only listening.
A passenger no one remembers boarding is spotted eating alone at the stern. By evening, no one can agree on what they look like.
The cook announces the freshwater stores smell of brine. He is adamant the barrels were sealed correctly when loaded.
Three off-duty sailors crouch at the bow, rolling dice using carved knucklebones. They stop and cover them when anyone draws near.
A length of rope on the forward mast has been tied into an elaborate series of knots that no one aboard claims to know. The knots are said to be a weather-working charm in some traditions.
The ship's cat refuses to go below decks and paces the rails all night, its eyes fixed on the water.
A sailor produces a set of beautifully illustrated cards and offers to tell fortunes. His readings are unsettlingly specific.
The first mate posts two extra lookouts and will not say why. She spends the afternoon consulting old charts alone in her quarters.
A persistent fog bank runs parallel to the ship all day, maintaining the same distance regardless of course changes.
An argument among the crew over rations comes to blows below deck. Order is restored, but the galley crew are sullen for the rest of the voyage.
A barrel of salted meat is opened to find the contents have entirely liquified. The smell is not of rot, but of something the cook cannot name.
At sunrise, the shadow of the mast falls the wrong direction for nearly ten minutes before correcting itself. Only a few crewmen notice.
A passenger falls ill with an unidentifiable fever. The ship's surgeon is at a loss. The patient mutters continuously in their sleep.
Someone has scratched a crude map into the planking of the lower deck. It does not match any coastline currently in view.
A crewman is caught trying to quietly throw a small bundle overboard. He claims it is personal property he no longer wants, but will not open it.
The helmsman reports the ship is not responding to the wheel normally. After an hour of investigation, a piece of carved driftwood is found wedged beneath the rudder.
The night watch hears singing from the water — a single voice, clear and close, with no vessel in sight.
Two crewmen separately report dreaming of the same port in the same night: a harbor they do not recognize, with red lanterns.
A stowaway is discovered in the hold: a child of about ten who speaks no language anyone aboard knows.
The rigging creaks in a rhythmic pattern during a windless stretch. The sailors exchange glances but say nothing.
A gull lands on the deck and will not leave. It pecks at one particular hatch repeatedly before finally being driven off.
A crewman is found to have been keeping a private log. When confronted, he destroys it rather than hand it over.
The ship's anchor, when brought up, is fouled with a chain of a different make — heavier, older, and attached to nothing.
One of the passengers asks too many detailed questions about where cargo is stored. The first mate quietly has them watched.
A small fire breaks out in the galley and is quickly contained. The cook says a lantern tipped, but the lantern is found unbroken and upright.
The crew grows restless and talks in low voices about turning back. No single reason is given, just a collective unease that settled overnight.
A crewman loses two fingers in a winch accident. He refuses to be seen by the surgeon and binds them himself with sailcloth.
The captain eats alone for the third day in a row. The officers will not discuss it.
Below decks, someone has painted a ward-sign in pitch on the interior hull. It is fresh. No one claims responsibility.
As dawn breaks, a second ship is spotted at anchor in otherwise empty open water. By the time any glass is trained on it, it is gone.
shipboardPositiveEvents: A school of silver fish leaps alongside the hull in perfect unison at first light — the sailors say it means clear skies for seven days.
The ship's oldest rope, frayed and due for replacement, holds a sudden hard gust without snapping. The crew takes it as a sign of the vessel's endurance.
A green flash is seen at sunset, just as the sun touches the horizon. The navigator calls it the rarest of omens and marks the logbook with a star.
A pair of white-winged terns circles the masthead three times before flying ahead on the intended course. The crew follows without hesitation.
Against all odds, a net hauled up from deep water contains a single coin — foreign, ancient, and still bright.
The sea turns a vivid deep blue just before dawn, the color sailors call the 'true water.' It is said to mean fortune for whoever sails through it.
A breeze picks up from exactly the right quarter, filling every sail and cutting half a day from the journey without anyone touching a rope.
A large sea turtle surfaces alongside the ship and keeps pace for over an hour. Old sailors refuse to call it anything but a blessing.
The ship's cat gives birth to a healthy litter of five. All five kittens are born with the same unusual white marking. The crew is delighted.
As the anchor is weighed in the morning, it brings up a snarl of kelp threaded with small luminescent organisms that glow in the pre-dawn dark.
Three dolphins ride the bow wake for an entire afternoon, never tiring. The mood aboard lifts noticeably.
A falling star traces a long arc directly ahead of the ship's course just after midnight. The helmsman steers toward where it fell.
A rainbow forms in the spray off the port bow despite there being no rain in sight. It stays for nearly half an hour.
The cook finds a cache of excellent preserved food tucked into a corner of the hold — far better quality than anything loaded at the last port.
A seabird no one can name lands on the deck and allows itself to be gently picked up. It sits calmly on the first mate's arm for an hour then flies off.
The ship's bell rings once on its own in the still of the night. The old hands say it means a safe arrival is coming.
Just before a squall hits, the clouds part directly overhead and the ship passes through unscathed while the storm rages on all sides.
A lookout spots a rare navigation star — not on any chart — burning unusually bright. The navigator plots by it and shaves a full day off the voyage.
The captain draws a favorable water sample from the deep: cold, clear, and slightly sweet. The crew calls it the taste of a safe passage.
A knot in the rigging that had no right to hold the tension it was under is found intact and perfectly seated. The bosun calls it uncanny.
A pod of whales surfaces just before dawn, their breath catching the first light. Each spout rises straight up into windless air — no bad weather coming.
The figurehead, long faded, seems somehow brighter in the morning light. None of the crew can explain it, but all of them notice.
A stowaway litter of rabbits is found in a supply crate — not eaten, not sick, and apparently very comfortable. The crew argues over names.
The ship's carpenter discovers a timber rot that was about to become critical — caught just in time to fix at sea with materials already aboard.
Stars reflect off the water so clearly on a calm night that the helmsman swears he could navigate by the sea itself.
shipboardNegativeEvents: Someone has driven a tenpenny nail into the underside of the helm. No one admits to it, and no one knows how long it has been there.
The figurehead's painted eyes have been scratched out. The crew found it this way at first light. No one heard anything in the night.
A barrel of drinking water is found to have a thin film of oil floating on its surface. The source cannot be determined.
Three distinct knocks sound from beneath the hull during a calm stretch. The sea around the ship is empty and the depth is vast.
The compass begins pointing slightly off true for a full day before correcting. The navigator makes note but offers no explanation.
The ship's log has a page missing — torn cleanly, not accidentally. The missing entries covered the last two nights.
The cook's fire goes out entirely and refuses to relight for several hours. The crew eats cold food and says nothing.
A rope from the lower rigging is found coiled on deck in the shape of a hangman's noose. No one will touch it until the first mate kicks it apart.
At midnight, every lantern aboard dims to nearly nothing simultaneously, then recovers. The oil levels are fine.
Two crew members fall ill on the same night with the same symptoms: cramped hands and temporary blindness. By morning they are recovered but shaken.
Something large passes beneath the ship during a calm, rocking it slightly. Whatever it is goes unseen.
The first catch of the morning haul produces nothing but black fish — the kind no one eats, for reasons no one entirely remembers.
A sharp smell like burning hair wafts from belowdecks for nearly an hour, but no source is found and nothing is scorched.
The night watch reports hearing their own name called from somewhere aft. On investigation, nothing is there.
A passenger's belongings are found scattered across the deck in the early morning, though the passenger is asleep in their bunk.
Rust appears overnight on an iron fitting that was clean and oiled the evening before. The rust has a distinctly red, almost blood-like hue.
A halo of pale light rings the moon all night. Old sailors' superstition says it means a death aboard before landfall.
The ship's cat disappears for a full day and is found hiding inside a sealed cask with no way in or out.
The rudder shudders once, violently, in a flat calm. When checked, the pintles and gudgeons are undamaged and properly fixed.
A crew member's shadow falls the wrong way relative to the sun for a few seconds. Three people see it. No one mentions it aloud.
Seabirds follow the ship in unusual numbers, none of them crying — just watching silently from the rigging and gunwales.
The water astern of the ship turns briefly dark, as if something enormous and pale turned just below the surface.
A coil of rope left on deck overnight has been neatly unknotted by morning. Every knot, carefully undone.
The smell of a specific port — one that no one can quite agree on — wafts across the deck for a full hour while completely at sea.
An inexplicable cold spot develops near the stern ladder. Animals refuse to walk through it. It persists until landfall.
shipwrecks: The keel of a vast merchant galley rises from the shallows at low tide, so thickly encrusted with barnacles and mussels it resembles a living reef. The hold is accessible, though chest-deep in opaque water. Crates inside are sealed, their contents unknown.
A longship lies upright on the seabed in only ten feet of water, preserved almost perfectly — no rot, no barnacles. It is as if it sank yesterday, though the style of construction is centuries old.
Two ships have sunk together, their hulls fused by some tremendous impact. Where they meet, the wood has warped into a jagged arch. A rope ladder hangs from each deck, each descending into the water.
A fast courier vessel lies on its side where it clearly rode a wave too far up the shore. Its cargo hatches have been forced, the contents removed. One sealed compartment in the bow remains intact.
The mast of a sunken warship breaks the surface here, its pennants still attached and barely faded. A crow's nest sits just above the waterline. Inside, wrapped in oilcloth, is a ship's log.

A peculiar craft built from materials no shipwright would recognize — some kind of dark, dense wood with no visible grain — sits half-buried in sand. Its hull bears no damage. It simply appears to have been abandoned here.

A trading cog rests in a rocky crevice, wedged so firmly it could not have arrived there by any ordinary current. Its cargo manifest, nailed to the interior cabin door, lists the contents of thirty-four crates. Seventeen are still present.

A fire-blackened warship lists against a submerged rock shelf. Its iron fittings are melted and resolidified into strange shapes. The fire that destroyed it clearly burned hot enough to melt iron — from within.
A slender scouting vessel lies on a sandbar, broken in half amidships. The break is perfectly clean, as if cut by an enormous blade. Neither half shows signs of collision with anything.
The upper deck of a sunken ship is just visible through twelve feet of clear water. It is completely covered in white sea anemones, flowering open in the current. The hold below them is in darkness.

A grand passenger vessel rests in forty feet of water, remarkably intact. Through its portholes, passengers' personal effects are still arranged on shelves and tables. The captain's log, in the wheelhouse, is open to its final entry.
The wreck of a fishing trawler sits in a sheltered cove, its nets still deployed and dragging in the current. The nets are filled to bursting — not with fish, but with smooth, identical river stones.
A small, fast ship has driven prow-first into the sea floor at a steep angle, stern pointing upward. The rudder is still intact. Whatever struck the bow struck it going downward, not across.
The bones of an extremely large vessel are scattered across half a mile of seabed, as if the ship exploded outward from some internal force. No piece of hull is larger than a rowboat.

A sleek vessel in the style of a northern raiding ship sits upright in twenty feet of water. Its oars are shipped, its shields racked, its provisions still aboard. Not a bone or personal item remains.

A barge laden with worked stone blocks has settled to the bottom, its cargo intact. The blocks are carved with imagery that no living scholar can identify. The ship's name, painted on the stern, is in a script not matching any known language either.
A small exploration vessel is pinned beneath an overhang of collapsed cliff face. The rockfall was recent. The ship was clearly there long before the cliff fell.
A heavily armed patrol ship rests in a natural harbour, scuttled deliberately — sea cocks open, hatches sealed. Its flag has been nailed upside-down to the stump of the mainmast.

The wreck of a pleasure yacht, built for luxury, rests in shallow water. Fine furnishings are still bolted to the bulkheads. A dinner table in the main saloon is still laid for six, the porcelain preserved but empty.

An ancient vessel has been so thoroughly colonized by coral that it looks more geological than nautical. Fish dart in and out of what were once gun ports. At its centre, a bronze ship's bell hangs intact, still attached to its beam.

A deep-water supply ship lies on a slope, slowly sliding deeper. Ropes have been tied around convenient outcrops to slow the descent. Someone was still aboard after the sinking — and trying to stop it from going further down.

A reed boat — an unusual and ancient construction — has partially sunk but refuses to fully submerge, held up by the natural buoyancy of its materials. It drifts slowly in a wide circle, unable to go anywhere, unable to sink.
Two small lifeboats from some larger, unseen disaster float upright and empty in a cluster, tied together with fresh rope. There is no wreck in sight. The nearest land is days away.
The hull of a mid-sized cargo vessel has been split open along one side, pried apart from outside. The damage pattern is consistent with something very large forcing itself in — or out.
A warship bearing the colors of a nation that ceased to exist two hundred years ago sits upright in shallow water, showing no evidence of the age it must be. Its cannons are loaded.
A smuggler's vessel lies broken against a hidden reef marked on no chart. Its hidden compartments, concealed behind false bulkheads, stand open and empty — ransacked, but not recently.

A large vessel has been dragged onto a beach and stripped methodically of every usable component. Hull planks, fittings, rigging, masts — all gone. Only the skeleton of the keel and a few ribs remain, scorched clean.

The wreck of a hospital ship, identifiable by its painted markings, lies in peaceful waters. Its medical stores are partially intact. So is the register of patients — over three hundred names, none of them corresponding to any crew.

A privateer vessel has been run aground and burned, the classic sign of a captain who preferred to destroy the ship than surrender it. Among the ashes in the burned-out captain's cabin, a sealed iron box that did not burn.

A vast, flat-bottomed cargo barge has sunk upright in shallow water, its deck just barely below the surface at high tide. At low tide, the entire deck is exposed — including a detailed map, carved into the wood, of a coastline none of the party recognize.
